+// specific language governing permissions and limitations under the License.
+
+#include "size.h"
+
+namespace ncnn {
+
+Size::Size()
+{
+    one_blob_only = true;
+    support_inplace = false;
+}
+
+int Size::forward(const Mat& bottom_blob, Mat& top_blob, const Option& opt) const
+{
+    top_blob.create(1, 4u, opt.blob_allocator);
+    if (top_blob.empty())
+        return -100;
+
+    top_blob[0] = bottom_blob.w * bottom_blob.h * bottom_blob.d * bottom_blob.c;

discussion_comments.comment_body:
onnx size outputs an integer tensor
The code implemented here by ncnn size will return a float tensor
For very large numbers, there is a loss of precision when using float
Will this cause difficulty in subsequent layer processing? For example, perform other addition, subtraction or modulo division operations on the result of size.

discussion_comments.comment_body: for things like rms norm etc, it might have a little impact. but, the issue is that the size of batches (which ncnn does not have by itself), will have this. other than devisions for normalizations, I dont think it has other impacts. but if necessary (which is better), I might change the types from float to int. should anything else be done to make it compatible with integers?

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I'm worried that your original intention was to return integer, but you accidentally returned float tensor. Now it seems that I have unnecessary worries.  :D

Regarding batch, the process of converting the model to ncnn will require that the batch is always 1. There may be special cases, but that will also be solved in the converter, such as intepret nchw to cdhw in ncnn as a workaround. The layer itself doesn't care about it

> The shrink operation logic appears incorrect. When the input value is within the threshold range (-lambd to lambd), it should return 0.f only for hard shrink. For soft shrink, values in this range should be shifted by bias. This single implementation cannot handle both shrink variants correctly.

check the onnx doc
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/main/docs/Operators.md#Shrink

get_coord() itself is capable for out-of-bound coord resovling.
padding_mode is not regarded for the three neighbor pixels here and is always treated as zero when out-of-bound. it shall not be like this way.
Is it possible that explicit padding (pad_left pad_right ...) could be applied before pooling section ?

According PyTorch implementation, it do not need padding params. [AdaptiveAvgPoolKernel](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/690eaf9c43f3f71051d90e9f47f25b014ce84681/aten/src/ATen/native/cpu/AdaptiveAvgPoolKernel.cpp#L33)

top_blob is three-dimension mat, num_output x h x channels
```
top_blob.channel(q)[p]=sum;
```
seems to be wrong

The h loop is missing here
